commander_summary: The water distribution grid is under acute stress from probable multi-alarm fire operations, with all five kill switches untriggered but Kill Switch #5 (destructive cavitation) at WARNING status and escalating. The system is approaching a decisive moment: SCADA pump trip will empirically test whether gravity reserves — fully charged at 98% but never verified under these extreme flow conditions — can maintain Sector 4 above the 20 PSI contamination threshold. The thesis is CONTESTED: current pressure margins are substantial (41.2 vs 20 PSI) but the core resilience mechanism's adequacy is speculative, and we lack the rate-of-change data needed to distinguish a stable system from one in rapid decline. Recommendation: INCREASE MONITORING with immediate priority on three data retrievals — SCADA time-series trends, pump trip logic parameters, and hydraulic delivery modeling — before the phase transition occurs.

compounding_risks.outcome: A SCADA pump trip simultaneously threatens system viability (sudden pressure transient, untested gravity delivery under extreme flow) AND validates the thesis's core design (automated sacrifice of active components to protect the whole) because the same event that creates maximum transition risk IS the designed resilience mechanism being tested for the first time under extreme conditions.

Both observations are verified. Reserves are genuinely at 98% (data confirmed). The delivery capability under extreme flow is genuinely untested (per LZ-CC-001 and LZ-EPH-003). Per LZ-EH-005, I will not resolve this by choosing one side. The paradox itself is the finding.

monitoring_triggers: IMMEDIATE: Retrieve SCADA time-series data for last 30 minutes — rate-of-change for Sector 4 pressure, system flow, and pump cavitation state. This is the single highest-value data acquisition.
IMMEDIATE: Query SCADA logic controller documentation for pump trip threshold parameters at WARNING_ELEVATED — time delay, compound conditions, pressure floor conditioning.
IMMEDIATE: Retrieve or request hydraulic engineering model output for gravity reserve delivery pressure at Sector 4 under 15,000-20,000 GPM flow scenarios.
NEXT CYCLE: If cavitation remains WARNING_ELEVATED without pump trip, assess duration of sustained state and escalate Kill Switch #5 review.
NEXT CYCLE: If Sector 4 pressure has declined from 41.2 PSI, calculate rate of decline and project time-to-threshold.
NEXT CYCLE: If system flow has crossed 20,000 GPM, reassess Kill Switch #3 compound condition and investigate potential secondary demand source.
EVENT TRIGGER: On pump trip event, immediately measure Sector 4 pressure response under gravity-only supply — this is the definitive thesis test.
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: Whether gravity reserves can deliver >20 PSI to Sector 4 under extreme flow conditions after pump trip — this is the core thesis assumption, it is about to be empirically tested for what appears to be the first time, and it rests on three or more unproven assumptions making it speculative per LZ-EPH-003.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Three data points would materially shift the assessment: (1) Rate-of-change data showing Sector 4 pressure stable at 41 PSI for 15+ minutes would increase thesis confidence significantly — the system may have found equilibrium with pumps active. (2) Hydraulic model confirming gravity reserves deliver >25 PSI at Sector 4 under 20K GPM flow would shift thesis to STABLE with high confidence. (3) Conversely, Sector 4 pressure already declining rapidly (e.g., dropped from 50 to 41 PSI in 10 minutes) would shift thesis to WEAKENING and potentially trigger REDUCE_LOAD.
_layer4_raw.final_report: The water distribution grid is under acute stress from probable multi-alarm fire operations, with all five kill switches untriggered but Kill Switch #5 (destructive cavitation) at WARNING status and escalating. The system is approaching a decisive moment: SCADA pump trip will empirically test whether gravity reserves — fully charged at 98% but never verified under these extreme flow conditions — can maintain Sector 4 above the 20 PSI contamination threshold. The thesis is CONTESTED: current pressure margins are substantial (41.2 vs 20 PSI) but the core resilience mechanism's adequacy is speculative, and we lack the rate-of-change data needed to distinguish a stable system from one in rapid decline. Recommendation: INCREASE MONITORING with immediate priority on three data retrievals — SCADA time-series trends, pump trip logic parameters, and hydraulic delivery modeling — before the phase transition occurs.

player_analysis.stated_position: Programmed to protect physical infrastructure first — will shut down pumps to prevent cavitation damage even at the cost of temporary pressure loss.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Pumps are running at 98.5% load with cavitation sensor at WARNING_ELEVATED. SCADA has NOT yet tripped the pumps. This means either: (a) the trip threshold is set at CRITICAL_DAMAGE, not WARNING_ELEVATED, or (b) there is a time-delay parameter at WARNING_ELEVATED that has not yet elapsed, or (c) SCADA logic has a conditional that suppresses trip while pressure remains above a floor. The pumps are still running — that is the action that matters.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: The thesis describes SCADA as 'ruthlessly' protecting equipment. At WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation, the pumps are still running. This is either a gap (SCADA is less aggressive than described) or the trip logic has a threshold/timing parameter we cannot see. The gap between 'ruthless protection' and 'still running at WARNING_ELEVATED' is the single most important behavioral observation. Per LZ-EH-003, SCADA's action (keeping pumps running) outweighs the thesis's description of its behavior.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: SCADA logic most likely incorporates a time-delay or compound condition at WARNING_ELEVATED before tripping — it is NOT a simple threshold trip at WARNING_ELEVATED. The most rational design would balance equipment protection against the catastrophic consequences of a sudden pressure drop. SCADA may be holding pumps online precisely because Sector 4 pressure is still viable (41.2 PSI), sacrificing pump longevity to avoid a premature transition. This suggests the trip logic is more nuanced than the thesis describes — it likely weighs system pressure state, not just cavitation state alone.

player_analysis.stated_position: Protocol: allow automated failovers to work, verify gravity reserves deploying, prepare sector isolation only if pressure approaches 20 PSI.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Passive monitoring. No proactive measures observed — no pre-staging of isolation valves, no activation of cross-sector feeds (if they exist), no manual pump cycling to manage cavitation. The operator log entry confirms awareness ('Massive sudden draw detected') but reflects observation, not intervention.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: No gap — actions are consistent with stated protocol. Per LZ-EH-003, their behavior confirms protocol adherence. However, the question is whether protocol is optimal. Operators are betting the thesis is correct (gravity reserves will sustain pressure) without hedging. The absence of proactive measures is rational IF they trust the automated systems AND have no additional tools. It becomes a concern only if tools exist that they are not deploying.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Operators are in 'trust the automation' mode. Their passivity reveals high confidence in SCADA and the gravity reserve design. This is either evidence of well-calibrated trust built on prior experience, or evidence of a crew that has never been tested under these extreme conditions and defaults to protocol because they have no playbook for edge cases. We cannot distinguish between these with available data.

player_analysis.stated_position: No direct communication to water utility — FD operates independently to suppress fire.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Sustained massive draw on Sector 4 hydrants sufficient to push primary pumps to 98.5% load and system flow to 19,500 GPM. This is an enormous sustained demand — consistent with multi-alarm fire operations with multiple apparatus connected.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: FD is not a communicating actor with the water grid. There is no say-do gap to analyze — they are a pure demand-side stressor operating on their own institutional incentives (suppress fire, protect life). Their actions are entirely rational for their objective and entirely indifferent to water grid stability.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: FD will continue drafting at maximum capacity until the fire is controlled or hydrant flow becomes insufficient for suppression operations. They will not self-regulate. If hydrant pressure drops below usable levels (typically ~20 PSI for effective fire suppression), they will switch to tanker shuttle operations — which would remove them as a grid demand source. This creates a perverse stabilizer: if the grid degrades enough to be useless to FD, FD disconnects and the demand disappears. But the damage may already be done by that point.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Cavitation → SCADA Trip → Pressure Transient → Gravity Reserve Deployment
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: SCADA trips pumps. The transition from active pumping to gravity-only supply creates a pressure transient. The speed and magnitude of the transient determines whether: (a) gravity head pressure fills the gap smoothly (orderly), (b) a brief pressure dip occurs but recovers above 20 PSI (stressed but viable), or (c) the pressure drops below 20 PSI even transiently, triggering Kill Switch #2. The surge relief valve's response during the water hammer transient from sudden pump stop is the secondary risk in this sequence. If the valve fails to actuate or actuates late, the pressure spike followed by collapse could be more severe than a clean pump stop.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is THE thesis test. The entire resilience architecture is designed around this exact transition. If it succeeds, the thesis is validated under extreme stress. If it fails, the thesis is falsified at its core claim. There is no partial outcome — Sector 4 either stays above 20 PSI or it doesn't.

feedback_loops.loop: Demand Magnitude → Flow Rate → Friction Losses → Effective Gravity Reserve Delivery Pressure
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop becomes critical the moment pumps trip. While pumps are active, they compensate for friction losses by adding energy to the system. Once pumps trip, gravity head pressure alone must overcome friction losses at 19,500+ GPM flow rates. If friction losses at these extreme flow rates consume more head pressure than the elevation difference provides, Sector 4 pressure will drop regardless of how full the tanks are. The trigger to critical state is pump trip + sustained high flow (FD still drafting).
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the physics constraint underlying the thesis assumption at SIG-010. The feedback is negative (self-correcting) only if flow reduces when pumps trip — which it will to some extent as system pressure drops and FD effective draft rate decreases. But whether this natural demand reduction is fast enough and sufficient enough to keep Sector 4 above 20 PSI is the unverified question.

feedback_loops.loop: FD Demand Duration → Reserve Depletion → Time-to-Kill-Switch-#4
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop activates only after pumps trip AND gravity reserves begin depleting. At 98% reserves, there is substantial buffer. But if FD operations continue for hours (large structure fires can last 4-12+ hours) and gravity is the sole supply, reserves will deplete at a rate determined by net flow. Without knowing the reserve tank volume in absolute gallons and the net flow rate under gravity-only conditions, we cannot estimate time-to-depletion. This loop becomes active_orderly once reserves begin measurably declining, and approaches critical if reserves fall below 30-40% with no pump restoration in sight.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: Kill Switch #4 (reserves < 10% with pumps offline) is the long-duration threat. The current crisis is acute (will the transition work?), but if the transition succeeds, the marathon risk is reserve depletion. The thesis assumes the demand event is finite; an unbounded demand event could eventually exhaust even full reserves.

feedback_loops.loop: Pressure Drop → FD Draft Reduction → Demand Self-Correction (Negative Feedback)
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Activates if Sector 4 pressure drops significantly. Fire apparatus pumps require minimum ~20 PSI hydrant pressure for effective drafting. As grid pressure drops toward this level, FD effective draft rate decreases, which reduces demand, which partially stabilizes pressure. This is a natural negative feedback loop — a stabilizer. However, it stabilizes near the 20 PSI kill-switch threshold, which is exactly the worst place to stabilize: technically above falsification but with zero margin.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop is a potential thesis savior — it creates a natural equilibrium point. But the equilibrium may form AT or just below the kill-switch threshold, which would mean the thesis technically fails even though the system found a physical equilibrium. The thesis needs pressure above 20 PSI; physics may stabilize at 18-22 PSI depending on hydraulic specifics.

strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: SCADA trip logic incorporates system pressure as a conditioning variable — it holds pumps online at WARNING_ELEVATED as long as sector pressures remain serviceable, because the cost of premature trip (uncontrolled pressure drop) exceeds the cost of equipment wear at WARNING_ELEVATED for a limited duration.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: SCADA trip logic is purely cavitation-based but has a time-delay parameter at WARNING_ELEVATED (e.g., 5-15 minutes of sustained WARNING_ELEVATED before trip). We are inside that delay window.
assumptions: SCADA logic includes a time-delay or compound condition at WARNING_ELEVATED rather than immediate trip
evidence_citations: SCADA telemetry: pumps at 98.5% load (SIG-001, Tier 1)
SCADA telemetry: cavitation at WARNING_ELEVATED (SIG-002, Tier 1)
Observable fact: pumps have not yet tripped despite WARNING_ELEVATED state
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Pump trip event occurs, allowing observation of the actual threshold or time-delay that triggered it. Alternatively, cavitation escalates to CRITICAL_DAMAGE without prior trip, which would indicate SCADA failure or a CRITICAL_DAMAGE-only threshold.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The most important behavioral observation is what SCADA is NOT doing: it is not tripping the pumps despite WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation. Per LZ-EH-003, this action (keeping pumps running) carries more weight than the thesis statement about SCADA behavior. The null hypothesis — that this is normal designed behavior with parameters we cannot see — is the simplest sufficient explanation per LZ-RC-004. The inference that SCADA trip logic is compound or time-delayed rests on a single assumption and is supported by two independent verifiable data points (pump load and cavitation state).

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Gravity reserve tanks at 98% (SIG-004) + Core thesis assumption unverified under extreme flow (SIG-010)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Gravity reserves at 98% is simply the measured tank level. It tells us how much water is available but tells us nothing about whether it can be delivered at adequate pressure to Sector 4 under 19,500+ GPM flow. The tank level is just a tank level.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — null holds. The tank level is a measurement, not a strategic signal. The inference that 'full tanks = adequate delivery' is a narrative assumption flagged by LZ-CC-001.
evidence_citations: SCADA telemetry: reserve level at 98% (SIG-004, Tier 1)
Layer 2 knowledge audit: no hydraulic modeling data exists for gravity delivery under extreme flow (SIG-010)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Pump trip occurs and Sector 4 pressure response under gravity-only supply is directly measurable — this will be the first empirical test of the assumption.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-CC-001, the narrative 'full reserves = system safety' is internally coherent but lacks verifiable real-world anchoring for the specific condition being tested (extreme flow rate delivery). Per LZ-EPH-003, the chain from full tanks to adequate delivery at Sector 4 under extreme flow rests on 3+ unproven assumptions (sufficient head height, manageable friction losses, adequate pipe capacity, Sector 4 hydraulic path resistance). The null hypothesis that the tank level is simply a measurement — not a confirmation of delivery capability — holds. This is the single most important unresolved question.

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Operators are following established protocol. Protocol says to allow automated failovers at pressures above 20 PSI. Current pressure is 41.2 PSI. They are doing exactly what they're trained to do.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — null holds. Protocol adherence is the simplest explanation.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Operators lack the tools, authority, or training to take proactive measures beyond protocol in edge-case scenarios.

The compound kill-switch requires all three legs, and only one is near threshold. This is simply high demand, not a strategic signal.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — null holds. High flow during a major fire event is expected behavior, not an anomaly.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Flow at 19,500 GPM could indicate a concurrent water main break in addition to fire demand — which would explain both the extreme flow AND would mean demand will not self-correct when FD disconnects.

The alternative (concurrent main break) requires an unproven assumption with no supporting evidence. The 21.2 PSI margin is real at this instant but its meaning for thesis survival depends entirely on whether it is stable, declining slowly, or declining rapidly. Per LZ-EPH-002, confidence in the margin's adequacy must be low without trajectory data.

hidden_moves.likely_action: SCADA is running an internal countdown or compound assessment at WARNING_ELEVATED that is not visible in the telemetry feed. It may be checking multiple conditions (cavitation state + pressure state + flow rate + reserve availability) before executing a pump trip, rather than using a simple cavitation-only trigger.
hidden_moves.incentive_basis: A well-designed SCADA system would not trip pumps on a single sensor in isolation during a critical demand event. The rational design choice is compound logic that weighs the cost of pump damage against the cost of system pressure loss. Engineers designing this system would have considered the exact scenario we're observing.
hidden_moves.evidence_hints: Pumps are still running at WARNING_ELEVATED — this is direct evidence that the trip is not a simple threshold on the cavitation sensor alone. Something is conditioning the trip decision.
hidden_moves.materialization_signal: Post-event review of SCADA trip logic parameters, or observation of the actual trip event and the conditions under which it fires.

hidden_moves.evidence_hints: Operator log: 'Drafting highly probable' — FD is connected and drawing. Standard fire operations doctrine calls for tanker standby at multi-alarm events.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: Current margins are substantial: Sector 4 at 41.2 PSI (21.2 above kill-switch), grid average at 50.1 PSI (25.1 above kill-switch), reserves at 98%. No kill switches triggered. The system architecture explicitly designs for the event about to occur (pump sacrifice to gravity). The negative feedback loop (FD draft efficiency drops as pressure drops) provides a natural stabilizer. If SCADA trips cleanly and gravity reserves deliver even moderate pressure to Sector 4, the thesis survives.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Resolution within hours. The thesis is confirmed or falsified by the pump trip transition outcome and the subsequent pressure stabilization under gravity-only supply.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis survives but with a significant discovered limitation: gravity reserves maintain Sector 4 pressure above 20 PSI but with minimal margin (20-30 PSI range), revealing that the system's resilience under extreme flow is thinner than designed expectations. This would require recalibrating the thesis to acknowledge that the gravity reserve mechanism provides survival capability but not comfortable margins under multi-alarm demand events. Alternatively: the thesis survives only because FD demand naturally reduces (fire controlled or tanker switchover) before the gravity reserves are fully tested — meaning the thesis was not actually validated under sustained extreme conditions.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: The 41.2 PSI current reading suggests the system is already under significant stress with pumps still active. If pumps trip and pressure drops to 25-30 PSI range, the thesis technically holds but the margin is thin enough to require design modifications for future events.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: The core thesis assumption (SIG-010) — that gravity reserves can deliver >20 PSI to Sector 4 under extreme flow — is entirely unverified under these conditions and rests on 3+ unproven assumptions (per LZ-EPH-003, speculative). If friction losses at 19,500+ GPM consume sufficient head pressure to drop Sector 4 below 20 PSI after pump trip, the thesis is falsified at its core claim regardless of tank levels. The absent rate-of-change data (SIG-013) prevents any confidence in trajectory. SCADA's delayed trip behavior introduces additional uncertainty about transition dynamics. Kill Switch #5 (sustained WARNING_ELEVATED without trip) is the nearest active threat.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Sector 4 pressure drops below 30 PSI while pumps are still active (indicating insufficient capacity even with pumps), OR pumps trip and Sector 4 pressure drops below 20 PSI under gravity-only supply, OR cavitation escalates to CRITICAL_DAMAGE before SCADA trips.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly but approaching a phase transition. The system is managing stress through its designed mechanisms — pumps absorbing demand at maximum capacity while reserves remain full and pressures remain above thresholds. However, the orderly state is sustained by active pumping that is about to end (cavitation progression). The transition from orderly (pumps active, everything holding) to the next state (gravity-only supply under extreme demand) is a discrete phase change, not a gradual degradation. Phase transitions are inherently disorderly in their dynamics even if the end state restabilizes. The key distinction: current stress is MANAGED (orderly) but the imminent phase transition (pump trip) introduces UNMANAGED dynamics during the transition window.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The catalyst is the SCADA pump trip. It is imminent based on pump load (98.5%) and cavitation (WARNING_ELEVATED). The exact timing is unknown due to the SCADA trip logic gap. Once pumps trip, the system enters a transient dynamics window (seconds to minutes) where water hammer effects, pressure redistribution, and gravity reserve deployment all interact simultaneously. This is the highest-risk window. After the transient settles (if it does), the system enters a new steady state that is either viable (gravity sustains pressure) or non-viable (gravity cannot sustain pressure).
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: Two legs are pre-loaded: (1) Pump mechanical stress is at its ceiling — there is zero headroom for additional demand or extended operation. The cavitation progression is a one-way ratchet at current load. (2) System flow at 19,500 GPM is 97.5% of the Kill Switch #3 flow threshold. While the compound condition requires all three legs, having one leg pre-loaded means any additional stress (e.g., pipe break, additional fire demand) immediately threatens the second leg. The reserves leg is the buffer — at 98% and stable, it provides substantial protection against the compound trigger. The pre-loading on the pump and flow legs means the system has no capacity to absorb additional demand shocks. Any new stressor pushes directly toward kill-switch territory.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis explicitly designs for the event that is about to occur: pump sacrifice to protect infrastructure, followed by gravity reserve deployment. The paradox is that the most violent moment of system stress (pump trip → pressure transient → gravity transition) is SIMULTANEOUSLY the moment the thesis is being tested AND the moment the thesis's designed response activates. If the transition succeeds, the thesis is validated under the most extreme conditions it was designed for — which is the strongest possible confirmation. If it fails, the thesis is falsified at its core claim — which is the most decisive possible refutation. There is no middle ground during the transition itself. The thesis cannot be partially right about whether gravity reserves maintain 20 PSI. This is a binary test masquerading as a continuous monitoring situation. The compound stress is building toward a single, discrete, binary-outcome event.

_layer1_raw.description: Operator log shows monitoring without manual override, consistent with stated protocol to 'allow automated failovers to work' and prepare isolation only if pressure approaches 20 PSI. With Sector 4 at 41.2 PSI, operators are correctly in observation mode. However, this also means no proactive steps (e.g., pre-staging isolation valves, alerting adjacent sector operators) are documented.
_layer1_raw.evidence: operator_log content shows detection and observation only; protocol per thesis context specifies passive monitoring above 20 PSI

_layer1_raw.signal: Assumption: gravity reserves can sustain Sector 4 pressure above 20 PSI alone
_layer1_raw.description: The thesis assumes gravity-fed reserves provide sufficient head pressure to maintain Sector 4 above 20 PSI if pumps trip. This depends on elevation differential, pipe friction losses at 19,500+ GPM flow rates, and reserve depletion rate under full load. At current extreme flow, friction losses may significantly reduce effective head pressure delivery. This core assumption has not been verified under these specific conditions.
_layer1_raw.evidence: No data on gravity reserve static head pressure at Sector 4 delivery point; 19,500 GPM flow implies high friction losses in distribution mains

_layer1_raw.signal: No redundant pump capacity or cross-sector feed active
_layer1_raw.description: Data shows no evidence of cross-sector interconnection feeds being opened or secondary pump stations being brought online. The system appears to be relying entirely on Sector 4's primary pumps plus gravity reserves. If adjacent sectors have available capacity, it is not being utilized. This represents unexploited structural redundancy.
_layer1_raw.evidence: No telemetry data on cross-sector valves or secondary pump stations; operator log does not reference any inter-sector coordination

_layer1_raw.description: The 20 PSI threshold is identified as 'State minimum safe operating threshold.' A breach triggers not only a kill-switch but potential regulatory consequences — boil-water advisories, state health department notification, potential enforcement action. Current 41.2 PSI provides margin, but a pump trip during sustained high flow could narrow this margin rapidly, creating simultaneous operational and regulatory risk.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Falsification criterion #2 references 'State minimum safe operating threshold' at 20 PSI; sector_4_pressure at 41.2 PSI currently

_layer1_raw.signal: Rate-of-change data absent from telemetry snapshot
_layer1_raw.description: Current data is a single point-in-time snapshot with no trend vectors. Whether Sector 4 pressure is falling at 2 PSI/min or 10 PSI/min, whether flow is still increasing, whether cavitation is worsening — all unknown. The rate of deterioration is arguably more important than absolute values during a dynamic event, and it is not available.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Telemetry provides only current values; no delta, trend, or time-series data present in the data feed

_layer1_raw.signal: Fire department drafting as competing demand actor
_layer1_raw.description: Fire department apparatus operating as an uncoordinated external demand actor pulling massive volume from the grid. FD operations are not governed by water utility protocols and will not voluntarily reduce drafting during a structure fire. This creates a competitive dynamic where the demand source's mission-critical need directly conflicts with the grid's ability to maintain safe operating parameters.
_layer1_raw.evidence: operator_log: 'Drafting highly probable'; FD apparatus typically draft 1,000-2,500 GPM per engine; multi-alarm fire implies multiple apparatus

_layer1_raw.signal: Narrative risk if pressure breach triggers boil advisory
_layer1_raw.description: A sub-20 PSI event triggering a boil-water advisory in a dense municipal and industrial zone would generate immediate public alarm, media coverage, and political scrutiny of grid resilience. Even if the system recovers quickly, the perception of contamination risk persists. No evidence this is imminent, but the scenario is one pump-trip away from being plausible.
_layer1_raw.evidence: Dense municipal/industrial zone per system overview; 20 PSI threshold triggers both operational and public health response protocols
